title: PCI-DSS 10.3 — Audit log integrity: tampering/deletion detection on CDE log source
exclusions: Does NOT prevent tampering; raises on attempts to clear, delete, or modify CDE audit logs.
evidence: Saved search 'pci_10_3_log_tamper' (every 10m) emits per event: host, action, actor, target_log, to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: PCI-DSS 10.3 Audit logs protected from modification — directly evidenced.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Clearing a Windows Security log on a CDE test host (EventCode 1102) fires the UC.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Normal log rotation via scheduled task (with matching ticket) does NOT fire.

dataSources: `index=windows` EventCode IN (1102,104); `index=linux` auditd log clears, syslog `rotated-by-hand`.
app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833)
spl:
search (index=windows sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security EventCode=1102) OR (index=windows sourcetype=WinEventLog:System EventCode=104) OR (index=linux sourcetype=auditd type=CONFIG_CHANGE op=remove_rule) earliest=-10m
| eval action=case(EventCode=1102,"audit_log_cleared",EventCode=104,"event_log_cleared",op="remove_rule","auditd_rule_removed",true(),"unknown")
| rename TargetUserName AS actor host AS host
| table host action actor target_log
description: PCI-DSS 10.3 requires audit logs be protected from modification and deletion. This UC fires on the exact telemetry those operations emit.
value: A cleared Windows Security log on a CDE host is a near-certain indicator of attacker presence. Closing the detection gap to 10 minutes saves forensics time.
implementation: (1) Forward Windows Security + System + Linux auditd to Splunk; (2) schedule UC every 10m; (3) hit opens a P1 to IR + isolates host; (4) quarterly IR drill validates response.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=windows` EventCode IN (1102,104); `index=linux` auditd log clears, syslog `rotated-by-hand`..
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Forward Windows Security + System + Linux auditd to Splunk; (2) schedule UC every 10m; (3) hit opens a P1 to IR + isolates host; (4) quarterly IR drill validates response.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
search (index=windows sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security EventCode=1102) OR (index=windows sourcetype=WinEventLog:System EventCode=104) OR (index=linux sourcetype=auditd type=CONFIG_CHANGE op=remove_rule) earliest=-10m
| eval action=case(EventCode=1102,"audit_log_cleared",EventCode=104,"event_log_cleared",op="remove_rule","auditd_rule_removed",true(),"unknown")
| rename TargetUserName AS actor host AS host
| table host action actor target_log
```

Understanding this SPL

**PCI-DSS 10.3 — Audit log integrity: tampering/deletion detection on CDE log source** — A cleared Windows Security log on a CDE host is a near-certain indicator of attacker presence. Closing the detection gap to 10 minutes saves forensics time.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=windows` EventCode IN (1102,104); `index=linux` auditd log clears, syslog `rotated-by-hand`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: windows, linux; **sourcetype**: WinEventLog:Security, WinEventLog:System, auditd. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Applies an explicit `search` filter to narrow the current result set.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **action** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Renames fields with `rename` for clarity or joins.
• Pipeline stage (see **PCI-DSS 10.3 — Audit log integrity: tampering/deletion detection on CDE log source**): table host action actor target_log


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table of events by action, time chart, single value 'clears in last hour'.
visualization: Table of events by action, time chart, single value 'clears in last hour'.
knownFalsePositives: Host-retirement scripts may legitimately clear logs — exclude only with scheduled-retirement ticket linked.
